chunks.text: \chapter {Public Access Tape Drives} \renewcommand{\thempfootnote}{\arabic{mpfootnote}} \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{footnote}{0} \index{backup} \index{archiving} {\it The information in this section is also available on-line if you have a

is section is also available on-line if you have an account on an Instructional machine. See the file /usr/pub/tape.drives for the most current information. } To facilitate backup and retrieval of software on Instructional systems, we will have one

oftware on Instructional systems, we will have one or more public tape drives available. Users may archive their files to tape as necessary. There are many different formats for getting or saving data to the tape drives. The most universal command

a to the tape drives. The most universal command is {\bf tar.}\index{tar@{\bf tar} command} To use a tape drive, login to the workstation the tape drive is connected to. Load the type of media you wish to archive to or restore from, then enter your

ish to archive to or restore from, then enter your commands. \section{Tape Drive Names}\label{tape names} The availability of individual tape drives may vary, however, each system with a tape drive should have a sign on the console specifying the

e should have a sign on the console specifying the name of the tape drive. The tape drives most likely will be referenced with one of the following device names: {\tt /dev/rmt0h} or {\tt /dev/rmt/4mn}. \section{8MM Tape Drive Indicators and Loadin

n}. \section{8MM Tape Drive Indicators and Loading Instructions} \renewcommand{\thempfootnote}{\arabic{mpfootnote}} \begin{quote} GREEN LIGHT off and RED LIGHT off:\\ \hspace*{.5in}Ready to insert/remove 8mm tape.\\ GREEN LIGHT on and RED LIGHT off:

move 8mm tape.\\ GREEN LIGHT on and RED LIGHT off:\\ \hspace*{.5in}8mm tape loaded and ready.\\ GREEN LIGHT on and RED LIGHT flashing:\\ \hspace*{.5in}8mm tape is in use (reading or writing).\\ \\ Loading 8mm tape into drive:\\ \hspace*{.5in}Push but

ding 8mm tape into drive:\\ \hspace*{.5in}Push button to open tape mechanism.\\ \hspace*{.5in}Load tape into guide slots with open face of tape inward.\\ \hspace*{.5in}Push tape door closed.\\ \\ Removing 8mm tape from drive:\\ \hspace*{.5in}Type {\b

ving 8mm tape from drive:\\ \hspace*{.5in}Type {\bf mt off} to eject tape from drive.\footnote{Type ``{\bf man mt}'' for more information about this command. You can also push the button to open tape mechanism, but this can be problematic so use of

mechanism, but this can be problematic so use of the {\bf mt} command is recommended.}\\ \hspace*{.5in}Remove tape from guide slots.\\ \hspace*{.5in}Push tape door closed.\\ \end{quote} \section{{\bf Tar} -- Multivolume Archiver} \index{tar@{\bf t

bf Tar} -- Multivolume Archiver} \index{tar@{\bf tar} command} \index{tape archiver} The tape archiver command, {\bf tar}, saves and restores multiple files and directories either to or from a single archive. A few of the more common options that c

archive. A few of the more common options that can be used with the command are:\index{tar@{\bf tar} command!options} \begin{description} \item [-C] Use to perform a directory change prior to archiving data. \item [c] Create a new archive on

hiving data. \item [c] Create a new archive on tape, disk or file. Writing starts at the beginning of the archive instead of after the last file. \item [d] Use the next argument as the name of the device. \item [f] Use the next argument as t

the device. \item [f] Use the next argument as the name of the archive. \item [h] Save a copy of the actual file on the output device under the symbolic link name. \item [r] Write the named files to the end of the archive. \item [t] List t

les to the end of the archive. \item [t] List the names of the files as they occur on the input archive. \item [u] Add the named files to the archive if they are not already there, or if they have been modified since they were last put in the a

ve been modified since they were last put in the archive. \item [x] Extract the named files from the archive. If the named file matches a directory whose contents had been written into the archive, the directory is recursively extracted. If no f

, the directory is recursively extracted. If no file argument is given, the entire contents of the archive is extracted. \end{description} \noindent To archive files from {\tt /home/user/code} and {\tt /home/user/data} enter: \medskip {\tt \% } {

{\tt /home/user/data} enter: \medskip {\tt \% } {\bf tar c -C /home/user/code . -C /home/user/data .} \medskip \noindent To retrieve files that were in the subdirectory {\tt /home/user/proj1} from the tape enter: \medskip {\tt \% } {\bf tar x /hom

he tape enter: \medskip {\tt \% } {\bf tar x /home/user/proj1/*} \section{Sample Commands to Use} The following examples show various ways to use the {\bf tar} command to write, list and extract tar archives from a tape device. \begin{tabbing} x

ar archives from a tape device. \begin{tabbing} xxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxxxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxx \= \kill To create a tar archive consisting of files with a directory\\ structure with verbose mode (showing files being written) to the tape:

se mode (showing files being written) to the tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar cvf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h} \>{\bf ./myDirectory}\\ \\ To create a second tar archive on the tape at a later time:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf rewi

\% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf rewind} \>Go to start of tape.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf fsf} \>Skip first tar archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar cvf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h} \>{\bf ./data} \>Create archive.\\ \>

dev/rmt0h} \>{\bf ./data} \>Create archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h} \>{\bf offline} \>Rewind and eject tape.\\ \\ To create two tar archives on tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar cvf} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf ./programs} \>C

r cvf} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf ./programs} \>Create first archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar cvf} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf ./data} \>Create second archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h} \>{\bf offline} \>Rewind and eject tape.\\

mt0h} \>{\bf offline} \>Rewind and eject tape.\\ \\ To list a tar archive from tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar vf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h}\\ \\ To read a tar archive from tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar xf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h}\\ \\ To list the third tar

xf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h}\\ \\ To list the third tar archive on a tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf rewind} \>Go to start of tape.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf fsf 2} \>Skip two tar archives.\\ \>{

bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf rewind} \>Go to start of tape.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf fsf 2} \>Skip two tar archives.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar xf} \>{\bf /dev/rmt0h} \>\>Extract third archive.\\ \\ To read the multiple tar arch

third archive.\\ \\ To read the multiple tar archives from tape:\\ \\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf rewind} \>Go to start of tape.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar xf} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>\>Extract first archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf m

\>{\tt \% } {\bf mt -f} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>{\bf fsf 1} \>Skip end of file on archive.\\ \>{\tt \% } {\bf tar xf} \>{\bf /dev/nrmt0h} \>\>Extract third archive.\\ \end{tabbing} \section{Directory Structure Considerations When Archiving} Depen

ry Structure Considerations When Archiving} Depending on how the directory structure was specified when the tar archive is created, the files will be placed in the appropriate directory when you restore files from a tar archive. Listed below is a gu

ore files from a tar archive. Listed below is a guideline for specifying directory structure when using the {\bf tar} command; each case is followed by an example. \begin{tabbing} xxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxxxx \= xxxxxxxxxxxx \= \kill \

ent directory.)\\ \>\>{ \tt \% } {\bf tar cvf /dev/rmt0h .}\\ \\ \bigskip \>./dir-name\>Specifies to start at the specified subdirectory.\\ \>or\>Extracted files will be placed in the current directory\\ \>dir-name\>under the specified subdirectory.

y\\ \>dir-name\>under the specified subdirectory. The subdirectory will\\ \>\>be created in the current directory if it does not exist.\\ \>\>{\bf This is the recommended way for archiving files}\\ \>\>(Listing archive shows ./dir-name as parent dir
ory)\\ \>\>Extracted files will be placed in the home directory\\ \>\>under any specified directory.\\ \>\>{ \tt \% } {\bf tar cvf /dev/rmt0h $\;\sim$/post}\\ \end{tabbing}
